verdicts.description: The taint flow traces $_SERVER['HTTP_HOST'] through sanitize_text_field() and into wp_safe_redirect(). The automated scanner correctly identified that HTTP_HOST is attacker-controlled and reaches a redirect sink, but failed to account for wp_safe_redirect() semantics.
verdicts.analysis: wp_safe_redirect() internally calls wp_validate_redirect(), which validates the redirect destination against an allowlist: the site home URL (get_option('home')) and any hosts registered via the allowed_redirect_hosts filter. If an attacker spoofs HTTP_HOST to 'evil.com', the constructed URL 'https://evil.com/path' would be rejected by wp_validate_redirect() because 'evil.com' does not match the registered site host. WordPress then falls back to admin_url() as the redirect target. The sanitize_text_field() call on HTTP_HOST is redundant for security (wp_safe_redirect handles it) but does not create a bypass. Additionally, HTTP_HOST header spoofing at the network layer is generally controlled by the web server configuration on a hosting platform like Hostinger, further reducing exploitability.
verdicts.exploitation_path: Not exploitable. wp_safe_redirect() blocks cross-host redirects.

verdicts.description: Second taint flow for the force-www redirect path. Same source ($_SERVER['HTTP_HOST']) and same sink (wp_safe_redirect()). Shares the same analysis as hostinger-open-001.
verdicts.analysis: Identical protection mechanism to flow 001. The 'https://www.' prefix prepended to the attacker-controlled HTTP_HOST before passing to wp_safe_redirect() does not change the outcome: wp_validate_redirect() will reject any destination host that does not match the site's registered home URL. The 'www.' normalization only affects substrings, not the host validation logic inside WordPress.
verdicts.exploitation_path: Not exploitable. wp_safe_redirect() blocks cross-host redirects.

attack_surface_notes.note: Compares $_GET['bypass_code'] directly against the stored bypass code (no sanitization before comparison). This is safe — it is a string equality check, not a sink. The bypass code is a 16-character random hex string (from Helper::generate_bypass_code(16)), making brute-force impractical. No issue.
All routes (get-settings, update-settings, regenerate-bypass-code) are gated behind permission_check() which requires current_user_can('manage_options'). Properly restricted to administrators.
Single AJAX endpoint dismiss_plugin_split_notice verifies wp_verify_nonce() against 'hts_close_plugin_split' before acting. Properly protected.

isset() check missing for $_SERVER['H_PLATFORM'] before boolean negation: '! $_SERVER['H_PLATFORM']'. This could trigger a PHP notice/warning on non-Hostinger environments but is not a security issue. The function returns early with a WP_Error, preventing further processing.

File writes are to ABSPATH . 'llms.txt' using WP_Filesystem abstraction. No user-controlled path components. The generate() function is only triggered by authenticated admin actions or internal WordPress hooks (post status transitions, option updates). No arbitrary file write vulnerability.

update_settings() iterates over known setting keys from PluginOptions::to_array() and casts values with !empty(). The bypass_code field is explicitly preserved from existing settings and never overwritten by request parameters. No setting injection possible. The do_action() on line 126 uses the dynamic hook name 'hostinger_tools_setting_{$field_key}_update' where $field_key is from the controlled set of PluginOptions fields, not user input.
